stem: The nurse is caring for a 6-year-old client with a severe asthma exacerbation. Based on the client's electronic health record, identify the actions most likely to improve their condition and the actions that are least likely or potentially harmful.

itemContext.sbar: 14:00 - S: 6-year-old male with known asthma here for worsening respiratory distress. B: History of exacerbations requiring nebulizers and steroids. Symptoms worsened throughout the day. A: Audible wheezing, anxious. RR 36/min, SpO2 88% on room air, HR 120/min. Diminished breath sounds with prolonged expiration. R: Need to begin immediate treatment per asthma protocol. Administering ordered albuterol nebulizer and oxygen. Will monitor response closely and prepare for potential corticosteroid administration. Please advise on any further orders.

tabs.title: SBAR
tabs.content: 14:00 - S: 6-year-old male with known asthma here for worsening respiratory distress. B: History of exacerbations requiring nebulizers and steroids. Symptoms worsened throughout the day. A: Audible wheezing, anxious. RR 36/min, SpO2 88% on room air, HR 120/min. Diminished breath sounds with prolonged expiration. R: Need to begin immediate treatment per asthma protocol. Administering ordered albuterol nebulizer and oxygen. Will monitor response closely and prepare for potential corticosteroid administration. Please advise on any further orders.

tabs.title: Vitals
tabs.content:

tabs.content:
LabValue

tabs.title: Radiology
tabs.content: Chest X-ray: Diffuse hyperinflation, no consolidation noted.

tabs.title: MAR
tabs.content:
MedicationDosageRouteFrequency
Albuterol2.5 mgNebulizerPRN q20 min
Oxygen2 LNasal CannulaContinuous
Prednisolone30 mgOralOnce daily

hoverRationales.Administer albuterol via nebulizer: This short-acting beta-agonist (SABA) causes rapid bronchodilation by relaxing airway smooth muscle. This action directly counteracts bronchospasm, improving airflow and gas exchange during an acute asthma exacerbation.
hoverRationales.Administer oxygen via nasal cannula: The client's SpO2 of 88% indicates significant hypoxemia. Supplemental oxygen is critical to correct low oxygen levels, reduce the work of breathing, and prevent systemic tissue hypoxia.
hoverRationales.Administer corticosteroids: Corticosteroids reduce airway inflammation, the underlying cause of the exacerbation. Early administration is crucial to decrease inflammation and mucus production, preventing a late-phase reaction and symptom recurrence.
hoverRationales.Administer an antihistamine: This is clinically flawed as antihistamines do not address the bronchospasm or inflammation of an asthma attack. Their use can also cause sedation and thicken respiratory secretions, potentially worsening the client's condition.
hoverRationales.Encourage deep breathing exercises: This is clinically flawed during an acute exacerbation. The client is in significant distress and cannot perform these exercises effectively; attempting to do so may increase anxiety and the work of breathing.
hoverRationales.Place the child in a supine position: This is potentially harmful as a supine position restricts diaphragmatic movement and decreases lung expansion. The client should be placed in a high-Fowler's or sitting position to maximize thoracic excursion and ease breathing.

choices.text: Place the child in a supine position

stemActionsLeft.text: Most Likely to Improve Respiratory Status

responses: Place the child in a supine position
